narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background, you've put in the effort to pursue law school, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.30—which is actually a bit above average—and your decision to attend full-time, just like the vast majority of students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 68.8%, leading to the tough decision to reject your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 1 school decile ranking of 4th, which falls below the typical 5.71 average and stood out as the biggest factor pushing the model toward expecting a bar failure. Your LSAT score of 35, lower than the average of 36.7, was the second most influential concern, further nudging the prediction in that direction. On a more positive note, your Year 3 decile of 5 was right around the average of 5.53 and actually helped pull the prediction toward success, showing some improvement over time. Your family income level and undergraduate GPA each played smaller roles in raising concerns, though your GPA was a relative strength compared to peers. Overall, compared to typical students who get approved, your early academic decile and LSAT were the key areas where you fell short of what the model sees as strong bar exam predictors. It's disappointing, especially when you've shown progress and strengths elsewhere, but this isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just one data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to or above average, strengthening your early-grade performance through additional coursework or explanations in future applications, and targeting schools with more holistic reviews. You might also explore part-time options or gap-year experiences to build a stronger profile. Keep pushing; many successful lawyers faced early setbacks like this. We're rooting for you to turn this around.
